headlineSummary: HN sentiment skewed positive for Google Gemini and OpenAI, with Anthropic more mixed-neutral and a close tie between Gemini and OpenAI, while Anthropic also drew several critical remarks [E1][E3][E4][E5][E6][E7][E8].

ranking.summary: Anthropic reads mixed-neutral: praise for Claude Code and the model solving a hard problem is offset by criticism over secret training data, trust concerns, and an experiment that still failed despite good tests [E4][E5][E6][E7][E8].

Google Gemini is clearly positive, driven by praise for its new video embedding capability and comments calling it well suited to the task, with additional positive mention from the frontier math discussion; it is in a close tie with OpenAI [E1][E2][E4].

OpenAI is positive on the strength of GPT-5.4 solving a frontier math problem and related praise for frontier-model capability, though one comment criticizes ChatGPT/OpenAI’s closed training-data approach; it is in a close tie with Gemini [E3][E4][E6].

evidence.excerpt: Show HN: Gemini can now natively embed video, so I built sub-second video search

annotations.rationale: The title presents a new Gemini capability as enabling a useful application, signaling favorable sentiment toward Gemini.

evidence.excerpt: Gemini is great for this type of use case out of the box.

annotations.rationale: Direct praise that Gemini performs well for the described task.

evidence.excerpt: Epoch confirms GPT5.4 Pro solved a frontier math open problem

annotations.rationale: The title attributes solving a frontier math open problem to GPT-5.4 Pro, a strongly favorable capability claim.

evidence.excerpt: "In this scaffold, several other models were able to solve the problem as well: Opus 4.6 (max), Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.4 (xhigh)." I find that very surprising. This problem seems out of reach 3 months ago but now the 3 frontier models are able to solve it.

annotations.rationale: The comment says Opus 4.6 could solve the problem and frames that as surprisingly strong progress.
The comment says Gemini 3.1 Pro could solve the problem and treats that as impressive progress.
The comment says GPT-5.4 could solve the problem and presents that as notable frontier-model capability.

evidence.excerpt: Claude Code is amazing. I use it everyday because it makes me so much more efficient at my job. But I also know that by using it I’m contributing to making my job redundant one day.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt strongly praises Claude Code's usefulness while also expressing serious worry about job displacement, yielding mixed sentiment.

evidence.excerpt: So not all future human knowledge is privately scraped by chatgpt and anthropic – kept as secret training data, only available to them.

annotations.rationale: The commenter criticizes ChatGPT/OpenAI for privately scraping knowledge and keeping it as closed training data.
The commenter criticizes Anthropic for privately scraping knowledge and keeping it as closed training data.

evidence.excerpt: It will only take one agent-led compromise to get some Claude-authored underhanded C into llvm or linux or something and then we will all finally need to reflect on trusting trust at last and forevermore.

annotations.rationale: The comment invokes Claude as a source of potentially malicious code, expressing distrust toward Claude-authored output.

evidence.excerpt: Anthropic's C compiler experiment didn't fail because of bad testing. Not only were the tests good, it took humans years to write the tests by hand, and the agents still failed to converge.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt directly cites an Anthropic experiment as failing despite strong tests, which is a negative assessment of capability.
